coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of copper(II) and pyrophosphate ion
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wen Jie
Liu, Shi Gang
Han, Lei
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: A simple ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing system for Cu2+ and pyrophosphate ion (PPi) detection is established based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) which are synthesized via one-step hydrothermal approach. In the strategy, 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), can be adsorbed on the surface of N-CDs through electrostatic interaction, which efficiently quenches the fluorescence of N-CDs, meanwhile, oxOPD provides a new emission peak at 553 nm. In virtue of the selective oxidative and chromogenic reaction of OPD with Cu2+, a dual-readout sensing system for Cu2+ is achieved. In addition, the redox and chromogenic reaction among them can be inhibited by PPi, which protects effectively the fluorescence of N-CDs from quenching. This sensing system exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+ and PPi over other analytes with a low detection limit of 23 nM and 0.7 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensing system displays a prospective application for quantitative assay of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric detection
Cu2+
PPi

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative detection of Cu2+ and PPi
Detection of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs. Inset: typical N-CDs with lattice parameter of 0.23 nm. (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. (C) XPS survey scan of N-CDs. Inset: atomic percentage of elements of N-CDs. (D) High resolution of C 1 s peaks of N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (a), N-CDs + Cu2+ (b), N-CDs + OPD (c), N-CDs + Cu2+ +OPD (d). [Cu2+] = 10 μM. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (a), N-CDs + PPi (b), N-CDs + Cu2+ + PPi + OPD (c), N-CDs + Cu2+ +OPD (d). [Cu2+] = 20 μM, [OPD] = 1 mM, [PPi] = 100 μM, pH = 7.4.

(A) UV − vis absorption spectra of N-CDs + oxOPD (a), oxOPD (b), N-CDs + OPD (c), OPD (d), N-CDs + Cu2+ (e), N-CDs (f), Cu2+ (g). (B) UV-vis absorption spectrum of oxOPD (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectrum of N-CDs. The oxOPD is the product of OPD oxidized by Cu2+ in all measurements. [Cu2+] = 20 μM, [OPD] = 1 mM, pH = 7.4.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system to different Cu2+ concentrations. The inset photographs show the corresponding color evolutions under ultraviolet radiation. (B) Plot of fluorescent responses to the various concentrations of Cu2+, and the linear range for Cu2+ is 0.08–10 μM (inset). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system to different Cu2+ concentrations. The inset photographs show the corresponding color changes under daylight. (D) Relative absorbance against Cu2+ concentrations within the range of 0–40 μM. The linear range for Cu2+ is 0.08–25 μM (inset).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system to different PPi concentrations. The inset photographs show the corresponding color evolutions under ultraviolet radiation of 365 nm. (B) Plot of fluorescent responses to the various levels of PPi, and the linear range for PPi is 1–20 μM (inset). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system to different PPi concentrations. The inset photographs show the corresponding color changes under daylight. (D) Relative absorbance dependent on the PPi concentration within the range of 0–100 μM, and the linear range for PPi is 1–20 μM (inset).
Ratiometric fluorescent responses to various metal ions. The concentration of Cu2+ is 20 μM, and the concentrations of other metal ions are 25 μM.
Schematic illustration for detection of Cu2+ and PPi by N-CDs with both ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric readouts.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of copper(II) and pyrophosphate ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) have been prepared successfully via one-step hydrothermal approach and used to detect sequentially Cu2+ and pyrophosphate ion (PPi) by ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout assay.

A simple ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing system for Cu2+ and pyrophosphate ion (PPi) detection is established based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) which are synthesized via one-step hydrothermal approach. In the strategy, 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), can be adsorbed on the surface of N-CDs through electrostatic interaction, which efficiently quenches the fluorescence of N-CDs, meanwhile, oxOPD provides a new emission peak at 553 nm. In virtue of the selective oxidative and chromogenic reaction of OPD with Cu2+, a dual-readout sensing system for Cu2+ is achieved. In addition, the redox and chromogenic reaction among them can be inhibited by PPi, which protects effectively the fluorescence of N-CDs from quenching. This sensing system exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+ and PPi over other analytes with a low detection limit of 23 nM and 0.7 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensing system displays a prospective application for quantitative assay of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained extensive attention for their extraordinary performances, which include tunable fluorescence, nontoxicity, and good stability. In view of these outstanding benefits, great advance has been made in many applications, such as optoelectronic conversion [1,2], sensing [3–5], medical diagnosis [6], drug delivery [7], and bioimaging [8–10]. Among them, it is noticeable to design CDs-based probes to detect proteins, small molecules, and ions. For example, Zhang et al. used CDs as a colorimetric sensor to detect Hg2+ [11]. Qian et al. reported a reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly in response to pyrophosphate ion (PPi) [12]. In addition, a growing number of CDs-based Cu2+ sensing methods were explored in recent years [13–17]. However, to serve as a prospective sensor for Cu2+ detection, there are some defects that hinder their practical applications. Firstly, the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of many reported CDs are low relatively. Secondly, the selectivity of some CDs for Cu2+ detection is not entirely satisfactory because of the coexisting Hg2+, Ag+ and Pb2+, which can also decrease the fluorescence intensity through electron transfer process. Finally, the accuracy of Cu2+ detection based on CDs is not good enough under some potential interferents in practical samples. Therefore, the design of an effective sensing strategy for Cu2+ detection based on CDs with high QYs and fascinating specificity is still in pursuit.

For this purpose, we firstly constructed a dual-mode CDs-based sensor with both ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readouts for stepwise detection of Cu2+ and PPi (Scheme 1 ). The N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by using adenosine as the precursor through an environmentally friendly process. The synthesized N-CDs owned strong fluorescence and good photostability. In the sensing strategy, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized by Cu2+ to form 2, 3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD). The fluorescence of oxOPD at 553 nm can remarkably increase with increasing levels of Cu2+, while the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched simultaneously by oxOPD in virtue of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). More interestingly, the oxidative and chromogenic reaction of OPD triggered by Cu2+ can be forbidden by PPi because of the formation of Cu2+-PPi complexes. Compared with a kind of output signal, ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing assays attract great attention because they show multiple output signals conveniently, and hence make results more reliable [18]. The dual-mode carbon dots-based sensor was employed for detecting Cu2+ and PPi with satisfactory results in environmental water samples, which indicates promising potential for practical applications.

Adenosine (A), thymidine (T), cytidine (C), and guanosine (G) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and tris-(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal ion solutions including Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ were prepared from MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, KCl, NaCl, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2·2.5H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, respectively. For Na4P2O7·10H2O, Na3PO4·12H2O, NaF, NaCl, NaBr, NaI, NaNO2, Na2SO4, Na2SiO3, CH3COONa and Na2CO3, no purification treatment was required. Tris−HCl buffers (20 mM, pH 7.2–9.0) were obtained according to an appropriate proportion.

The ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence measurements were conducted with a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Suzhou Shimadzu Instrument Co., Ltd., China) and an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken on an EscaLab-250 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα source (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL-2100 system (JEOL Ltd.).

N-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Adenosine (0.0134 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL), and the solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. The brownish-yellow transparent solution was obtained by removing the deposit in a centrifuge (10 000 rpm, 10 min). The resulting solution was preserved in 4 °C for further experiments. Additionally, using 0.0122 g of cytidine, 0.0142 g of guanosine, and 0.0121 g of thymidine as the precursor individually, three types of N-doped carbon dots were also prepared.

The obtained N-CDs were firstly diluted 3 times with ultrapure water. For the detection of Cu2+, 950 μL of Tris−HCl buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4), 10 μL of 100 mM OPD, and 10 μL of Cu2+ solution with different levels were mixed. The resulting mixtures were incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. After that, 30 μL of N-CDs was introduced to the above mixtures and equilibrated for 2 min at ambient temperature. Finally, the fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded for quantitative detection of Cu2+. For the detection of PPi, 50 μL of varied concentrations of PPi were respectively mixed with 10 μL of 1 mM Cu2+ solution and then the mixtures were added to the aqueous solution containing OPD (10 μL, 100 mM) and Tris−HCl buffer (900 μL, 20 mM, pH 7.4). The above solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. Afterward, 30 μL of N-CDs was added to the above solutions and equilibrated for 2 min at ambient temperature before the spectral measurements.

The morphology of N-CDs was characterized by TEM. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared N-CDs are spherical and well monodispersed in water solution. A lattice spacing of 0.23 nm is observed (inset of Fig. 1A), which is approximately graphite (100) facet [19–22]. In addition, the size of N-CDs is relatively small ranging from 3.6 to 6.0 nm with the average diameter of 4.8 nm (Fig. S1). The result suggested that N-CDs were prepared successfully.

Surface analysis of N-CDs was carried out by the FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. The FT-IR spectrum was initially utilized to identify the bonding manner of N-CDs. A strong absorption peak located at 3447 cm−1 is the overlap of NH and OH stretching vibrations, and the bands at 1661, 1416 and 1124 cm−1 indicate the existence of CO, CN, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1B). Additionally, The XPS was utilized to further confirm surface structure of N-CDs. Fig. 1C shows that there are three elements in N-CDs: 66% of carbon, 15% of nitrogen, and 19% of oxygen. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) at binding energies of 287.8, 296.1, 285.0, and 284.6 eV can be readily recognized, which are ascribed to CO, CO, CN, and CC, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2) was attributed to three contributions, corresponding to 398.6 eV for CNH, 399.4 eV for N−H, and 400.1 eV for CN. The surface components of N-CDs characterized by XPS are consistent with those of FT-IR analysis, which demonstrate the presence of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups [23,24]. Excitation-dependent behavior of N-CDs was found (Fig. S3), which was different from the excitation-independent property, probably owing to the surface state defects [25,26]. The maximum emission of N-CDs was located at 412 nm with 330 nm excitation. From the inset in Fig. S3, the N-CDs emitted a bright blue fluorescence under the 365 nm irradiation.

By separately employing different nucleosides including adenine, cytidine, thymidine, and guanosine as the precursor, N-doped carbon dots were successfully prepared after heating at 200 °C for 9 h. The quantum yields of these carbon dots and the detailed information of corresponding nucleosines are summarized in Table S1. N-CDs synthesized with adenine show the highest quantum yield up to 11.3%. Therefore, N-CDs have promise to serve as an excellent fluorescent probe.

The obtained N-CDs exhibit great stability. From Fig. S4 A, slight change of fluorescence was found under 365 nm UV light exposure for 60 min, suggesting that N-CDs possessed good photostability. As described in Fig. S4B, N-CDs were still stable for 90 days when preserved at 4 °C. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs had no obvious decline though the concentration of NaCl ran up to 4.95 M (Fig. S4C), indicating that N-CDs were quite stable in a medium with comparatively high ionic strength. In addition, the effect of pH on the fluorescence of N-CDs is displayed in Fig. S4D, illustrating that the change of pH values had little influence on the fluorescence of N-CDs.

To estimate the feasibility of Cu2+ detection, we examined the effects of some related factors. As seen from Fig. 2 A, there was a negligible decrease of the fluorescence of N-CDs when N-CDs were incubated with only Cu2+ (curve b) or OPD (curve c). However, the fluorescence of N-CDs at 412 nm decreased dramatically, and a new emission at 553 nm appeared when OPD and Cu2+ coexisted simultaneously (curve d). These phenomena suggested that both Cu2+ and OPD had little influence on the fluorescence of N-CDs and OPD was an essential part for Cu2+ detection. The proposed strategy supplied the basis for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cu2+. In addition, OPD can be oxidized by Cu2+, and the resultant oxOPD has an apparent absorption band at 417 nm which is unaffected by N-CDs (Fig. S5). As a result, an effective dual-readout sensing platform was constructed and used for Cu2+ detection.

To assess the feasibility of our strategy for detecting PPi, the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of some related systems including N-CDs, N-CDs + PPi, N-CDs + Cu2+ + PPi + OPD, and N-CDs + Cu2+ + OPD were measured. Obviously, the fluorescence of N-CDs almost did not change when PPi was added into the reaction system, suggesting that PPi had little influence on the fluorescence of N-CDs (Fig. 2B). Moreover, premixing of PPi and Cu2+ can inhibit the oxidative reaction of OPD, leading to the obvious decrease in absorption near 417 nm (Fig. S5). Accordingly, a convenient dual-readout assay can also be designed to detect PPi.

To investigate the possible fluorescent quenching mechanism of N-CDs caused by oxOPD, a series of measurements have been carried out. Among different fluorescent quenching mechanisms, static quenching needs the strong interaction and formation of a ground-state complex between the fluorophore and quencher, which usually changes the absorption spectra [27]. We firstly measured the UV − vis absorption spectra of several substances and systems (N-CDs + oxOPD, oxOPD, N-CDs + OPD, OPD, N-CDs + Cu2+, N-CDs and Cu2+), which only exhibited characteristic absorption peak of oxOPD at 417 nm, and no other new absorption peak occurred in the system (Fig. 3 A). This result revealed that no new complexes were formed [28], which undoubtedly excluded the presence of static quenching. As shown in Fig. 3B, a large spectral overlap between absorption of oxOPD and emission of N-CDs indicated that the fluorescence quenching might be caused by FRET [29–32]. For further proving the possible quenching mechanism, the lifetimes of N-CDs were investigated in the absence and presence of oxOPD. From Fig. S6A, with the addition of oxOPD, the lifetime of N-CDs obviously decreased, suggesting a dynamic quenching process. Thus, the fluorescence quenching might be induced by FRET [33]. For FRET, the distance between the fluorescence acceptor and donor should be shorter than 10 nm. From Fig. S6B, the zeta-potential of N-CDs was −7.68 mV, whereas it turned to + 1.96 mV in the presence of oxOPD, suggesting that the oxOPD can be bonded on N-CDs surface. The distance between oxOPD and N-CDs was less than 10 nm due to the strong electrostatic interaction of them. Hence, the FRET could occur in the sensing system [34]. Based on above analysis, the possible fluorescent quenching mechanism is ascribed to FRET. For PPi detection, the redox reaction of Cu2+ and OPD can be forbidden by PPi due to the intense affinity between PPi and Cu2+, thus the fluorescence of N-CDs can not be quenched [35–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Cu2+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by oxOPD was firstly investigated by monitoring the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F553/F412) of the system. It was obvious that a steady ratio (F553/F412) was acquired after incubating 2 min (Fig. S7). Thus, 2 min was set as the optimal incubation time for Cu2+ detection.

As the Cu2+ concentration increased (0, 0.08, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 μM), the fluorescence at 412 nm progressively decreased while that at 553 nm increased (Fig. 4 A). As illustrated in inset photograph of Fig. 4A, upon exposure to varied concentrations of Cu2+, an obvious fluorescence color change from blue to orange to yellow can be easily found by bare eyes under a 365 nm UV lamp. The ratio (F553/F412) was linear response to Cu2+ concentrations from 0.08 to 10 μM (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ is 23 nM (S/N = 3). From Fig. 4C, With increasing Cu2+ level, the absorption band at 417 nm gradually increased and the color apparently changed from colorless to yellow (inset photograph in Fig. 4C). And inset of Fig. 4D shows the calibration curve based on the absorbance at 417 nm versus Cu2+ concentration from 0.08 to 25 μM. The sensitivity of our proposed strategy is lower than those of most reported methods listed in Table S2.

To date, using metal ion complexes as binding sites or media has been recognized to be a much effective method for PPi detection [38,39]. Similar to Fe3+ and Al3+, Cu2+ can bind to PPi through Cu–O–P bonds [40,41]. The intense affinity between Cu2+ and PPi can suppress the oxidation of OPD and reduce the absorption/fluorescence spectra enhancement when PPi was premixed. Additionally, the level of Cu2+ plays a crucial role in the determination of PPi. Taking both sensitivity and linear range of PPi detection into accounts, the final concentration of Cu2+ in PPi assay was set to be 10 μM. From Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity at 412 nm progressively increased while that at 553 nm decreased after introducing various concentrations of PPi (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100 μM). Meanwhile, upon exposure to varied concentrations of PPi, the color of the system changed gradually from yellow to orange to purple under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset photograph in Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B illustrates that a good linear relationship was found between the ratio (F553/F412) and PPi concentration from 1 to 20 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for PPi is 0.7 μM (S/N = 3). From Fig. 5C, the absorbance at 417 nm gradually decreased with an increase in PPi concentration. As a result, the color of the system evidently changed from yellow to colorless (inset photograph in Fig. 5C). And the absorbance at 417 nm was linearly dependent on PPi concentration from 1 to 20 μM (Fig. 5D). The sensitivity of our strategy for PPi detection is comparable to or slightly better than many reported methods [42–45].

The complicacy of practical samples expresses a large challenge for quantitative detection of Cu2+ not only in sensitivity but more significantly in specificity. Therefore, 14 different metal ions, including Mg2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Fe3+ and K+ were selected to evaluate their impact on detection of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 6 , these potentially interfering ions had negligible effect on ratiometric fluorescence measurement for target Cu2+. It was worth mentioning that Ag+ can also oxidize OPD to oxOPD owing to it more powerful oxidation than Cu2+ [46,47]. Nevertheless, a large number of Cl− in Tris−HCl buffer would keep from the interference of Ag+ [48,49].

To confirm the specific interaction of PPi with Cu2+, we prepared some common anions including PO4 3−, Ac−, I−, Br−, Cl−, F−, NO2 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2− and SiO3 2−. As seen from Fig. S8, insignificant changes were obtained when these anions were mixed with PPi. Thus, the above results substantially demonstrate that our proposed strategy has good specificity toward PPi detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability of our proposed strategy, the concentrations of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples were determined by both ratiometric fluorescent and colormetric methods. Water samples used for Cu2+ detection came from the tap water of laboratory in Southwest University. As exhibited in Fig. S9 and Table S3, Cu2+ was not found in the water sample. After spiked with Cu2+ standard solutions, the recoveries of Cu2+ were in the range from 95.0% to 108.3% and 98.5% to 108.6%, respectively, indicating ratiometric fluorescence and colormetric sensing strategy was applicable for the detection of Cu2+ in practical samples. In addition, satisfactory results for PPi detection were also obtained in urine samples. As shown in Fig. S10 and Table S4, the PPi was not detectable in the urine sample. The standard addition experiments display that the recoveries were in the range of 97.8% to 114.1% and 96.9% to 104.2%, indicating that the developed ratiometric fluorescence and colormetric sensing strategy can also be applicable for the detection of PPi in practical applications.

In conclusion, we have rationally developed a dual-signal (ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric) assay based on N-CDs for highly effective detection of Cu2+ and PPi. The developed method possesses the following advantages: (1) the N-CDs exhibiting good optical and chemical stabilities were synthesized through a facile and eco-friendly procedure; (2) this sensing system with ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual modes can show multiple output signals and makes detection results more reliable; (3) the proposed carbon dots-based dual-readout strategy possesses good potential for detection of Cu2+ and PPi in practical samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTC-2015jcyjB50001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.012.

ce:section.ce:label: The Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile access to versatile fluorescent carbon dots toward light-emitting diodes
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Adenosine-derived doped carbon dots: from an insight into effect of N/P co-doping on emission to highly sensitive picric acid sensing
Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultrabright N/P codoped carbon dots and its application in biological samples
In vivo cancer targeting and imaging with semiconductor quantum dots
Self-assembled hybrids of fluorescent carbon dots and PAMAM dendrimers for epirubicin delivery and intracellularimaging
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
Green preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from silkworm chrysalis for cell imaging
Carbon nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting mercury ions in aqueous media and living cells
A reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for detection of pyrophosphate ion
Carbon nano-dots as a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout probe for the detection of arginine and Cu2+ and its logic gate operation
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon quantum dots using waste polyolefins residue for Cu2+ ion sensing and live cell imaging
Yellow-visual fluorescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke for the efficient detection of Cu2+ ions
Dual-colored carbon dot ratiometric fluorescent test paper based on a specific spectral energy transfer for semiquantitative assay of copper ions, ACS Appl
Europium luminescence used for logic gate and ions sensing with enoxacin as the antenna
Toward efficient orange emissive carbon nanodots through conjugated sp2-domain controlling and surface charges engineering
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Facile synthesis of biocompatible N, S-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and ion detecting
Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots
Inner filter with carbon quantum dots: a selective sensing platform for detection of hematin in human red cells
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid
Luminescent europium-dipicolinic acid nanohybrid for the rapid and selective sensing of pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase activity
FRET effect between fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles and MnO2 nanosheets and its application for sensitive sensing of alkaline phosphatase
A ratiometric fluorescence universal platform based on N, Cu codoped carbon dots to detect metabolites participating in H2O2-generation reactions
A label-free fluorescence sensing approach for selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous Solution using graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Water-Soluble Nonconjugated polymer nanoparticles with strong fluorescence emission for selective and sensitive detection of nitro-explosive picric acid in aqueous medium
A sensitive polymer dots-manganese dioxide fluorescent nanosensor for “turn-on” detection of glutathione in human serum
A facile synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogendoped graphene quantum dots for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution
A multifunctional polymer combining the imidazole and zwitterion motifs as a biocompatible compact coating for quantum dots
Fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout assay for inorganic pyrophosphatase with Cu2+-triggered oxidation of o-phenylenediamine
Competitive coordination of Cu2+ between cysteine and pyrophosphate ion: toward sensitive and selective sensing of pyrophosphate ion in synovial fluid of arthritis patients
Fluorometric method for inorganic pyrophosphatase activity detection and inhibitor screening based on click chemistry
A highly sensitive gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric probe for pyrophosphate using a competition assay approach
Synthesis of functionalized fluorescent gold nanoclusters for acid phosphatase sensing
Geometry, charge distribution, and surface speciation of phosphate on goethite
Cysteine-directed fluorescent gold nanoclusters for the sensing of pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase

Synthesis of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles by Michael addition reaction as an analysis platform to detect iron ions and pyrophosphate efficiently and construction of an IMPLICATION logic gate
Reversible fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for real-time acid phosphatase activity monitoring
Highly sensitive real-time assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on the fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Label-free fluorescence detection of mercury ions based on the regulation of the Ag autocatalytic reaction
A nanoparticle autocatalytic sensor for Ag+ and Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity and its application in test paper
Core/shell Cu@Ag nanoparticle: a versatile platform for colorimetric visualization of inorganic anions
Ratiometric fluorescence monitoring of cerebral Cu2+ based on coumarin-labeled DNA coupled with the Cu2+-induced oxidation of o-phenylenediamine

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wen Jie Zhang is an MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their chemosensors application.
Shi Gang Liu is a PhD candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interests include photochemistry and photoanalytical chemistry.
Lei Han is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His research interests are the developments of chemical sensors and biosensors.

Hong Qun Luo is a full professor of chemistry at Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.

Nian Bing Li is a full professor of chemistry at Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are focused on size controlled synthesis, characterization, and size-dependent property study of metal nanocusters, nanobiosensing, and electrochemisty.
